attrs.alt: Azure Role Definition structure and built-in role hierarchy

content.text: What Is a Role Definition

A role definition is a collection of permissions expressed as allowed and excluded operations on Azure resources. Every role assignment links a security principal, a role definition, and a scope. The role definition answers the question: what can the assigned principal do? Azure ships over 400 built-in role definitions covering common access patterns. When none fit, you create a custom role with exactly the operations your use case requires.

Every role definition has the same JSON structure regardless of whether it is built-in or custom. Understanding the five core fields — Actions, NotActions, DataActions, NotDataActions, and AssignableScopes — is the foundation of crafting precise, least-privilege roles.
Role Definition Anatomy

A role definition is a JSON document with five permission arrays and metadata. The most important distinction is between Actions (control plane) and DataActions (data plane). Control plane operations manage the resource itself — creating a storage account, reading its properties, deleting it. Data plane operations act on the data inside the resource — reading a blob, writing a Key Vault secret, querying a database row.

# View the full JSON of any built-in role definition
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor' | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10

# The JSON structure of a role definition:
# {
#   "Name": "Contributor",
#   "Id": "b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c",
#   "IsCustom": false,
#   "Description": "Grants full access to manage all resources...",
#   "Actions": [ "*" ],
#   "NotActions": [
#     "Microsoft.Authorization/*/Delete",
#     "Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write",
#     "Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess/Action",
#     "Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/write",
#     "Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/delete",
#     "Microsoft.Compute/galleries/share/action"
#   ],
#   "DataActions": [],
#   "NotDataActions": [],
#   "AssignableScopes": [ "/" ]
# }

# Key insight: Actions: ['*'] + NotActions: ['Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write']
# = everything EXCEPT writing role assignments. NotActions does NOT create a deny rule;
# it simply subtracts operations from the effective Actions set.
Field
Type
Purpose
Actions
string[]
Control plane operations granted. Supports wildcards: Microsoft.Compute/*, *.
NotActions
string[]
Operations subtracted from Actions. NOT a deny — another role can still grant these.
DataActions
string[]
Data plane operations granted. Examples: blob read, secret get, table query.
NotDataActions
string[]
Operations subtracted from DataActions. Same semantics as NotActions.
AssignableScopes
string[]
Management groups, subscriptions, or resource groups where this role can be assigned.

NotActions does not create a deny rule. If user A has Role X (which NotActions the delete operation) and Role Y (which Actions the delete operation), the delete is permitted. Use Deny Assignments to truly block an operation regardless of roles.
Operations and Wildcards

Each operation string follows the pattern: ProviderNamespace/ResourceType/Action. Wildcards (* ) match any segment. Azure Resource Manager evaluates the effective permissions by computing the union of all assigned roles' Actions, then subtracting the union of all NotActions. Data plane evaluation follows the same logic independently on DataActions and NotDataActions.

# List all operations available for a specific resource provider
Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.Storage/*' | Select-Object OperationName, IsDataAction

# Filter to data plane operations only
Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.Storage/*' | Where-Object { $_.IsDataAction -eq $true } |
    Select-Object Operation, Description | Format-Table -AutoSize

# List all operations for Key Vault
Get-AzProviderOperation 'Microsoft.KeyVault/*' | Format-Table Operation, IsDataAction -AutoSize

# Wild-card patterns in Actions:
# 'Microsoft.Compute/*'              -> all VM operations
# 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*' -> all ops on VMs specifically
# 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read' -> only the read operation
# '*'                                -> all operations across all providers

# IsDataAction = true means the operation accesses data, not the resource itself.
# Example: Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read
# This reads blob content — it requires DataActions permission, not just Actions.
Common Built-in Roles

Azure's four foundational built-in roles cover the most common access patterns. Use them before considering a custom role — custom roles add management overhead and can become stale as Azure adds new operations.
Role
Role ID
Key Permissions
Typical Use
Owner
Actions: * (all control + data plane)
Subscription owner, emergency access
Contributor
Actions: * minus authorization writes
Developers, automation with no RBAC mgmt needs
Reader
acdd72a7-...
Actions: */read only
Auditors, monitoring agents, read-only dashboards
User Access Administrator
Microsoft.Authorization/* only
Teams who manage RBAC but should not touch resources
Owner is the only built-in role that allows assigning roles to others — Contributor explicitly NotActions Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write.
Reader grants read on resource metadata (names, config, SKUs) but does NOT grant read on data inside resources (blob content, secrets, database rows) — those require DataActions.
User Access Administrator combined with Reader is a common pattern for RBAC governance teams: they can see everything and manage access, but cannot modify resources.
Creating and Managing Custom Roles

Custom roles let you express exact least-privilege access. The most practical starting points are: start from scratch with specific operations, or clone a built-in role and trim it. Custom roles are tenant-scoped objects but are only assignable within their AssignableScopes. The limit is 5,000 custom role definitions per tenant.

# Create a custom role that allows starting/stopping VMs but not deleting them
$roleJson = @'
{
  "Name": "VM Operator",
  "IsCustom": true,
  "Description": "Can start and stop VMs but cannot create, delete, or resize them.",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/powerOff/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/deallocate/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/instanceView/read"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
  ]
}
'@

# Create the custom role from JSON
$role = $roleJson | ConvertFrom-Json
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Update an existing custom role (add an operation)
$existingRole = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'VM Operator'
$existingRole.Actions.Add('Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions/read')
Set-AzRoleDefinition -Role $existingRole

# Clone a built-in role as starting point for a custom role
$base = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Reader'
$base.Id = $null
$base.Name = 'Reader Plus Storage Write'
$base.IsCustom = $true
$base.Description = 'Read everything + write to blob storage'
$base.Actions.Add('Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/write')
$base.AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/<sub-id>')
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $base
Always restrict AssignableScopes to the narrowest scope where the role will actually be used. A custom role scoped to / (root) is visible and assignable everywhere.
When you update a custom role's operations, existing role assignments automatically inherit the new permissions — no reassignment needed.
Custom roles cannot be deleted while active assignments exist. Use Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName to find and remove them first.
Version-control your custom role JSON files alongside your IaC — role definitions change and you need change history.
DataActions and Data Plane Access

DataActions and NotDataActions control access to the data inside a resource rather than the resource itself. The classic example: a user with Contributor on a storage account can manage the account (change settings, add containers) but cannot read blob content unless they also have a role with the appropriate DataActions. This separation allows fine-grained data access without granting resource management capabilities.

# DataActions control data-plane access — reading/writing blob content,
# Key Vault secrets, SQL rows — as opposed to the resource metadata.

# Example: Storage Blob Data Reader built-in role
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Storage Blob Data Reader' | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5
# DataActions: [
#   'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read'
# ]
# Actions: [
#   'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/read',
#   'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/read'
# ]

# Create a custom role with blob read DataAction
$customDataRole = @{
    Name = "Blob Auditor"
    IsCustom = $true
    Description = "Read blob content and container metadata only"
    Actions = @(
        "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/read",
        "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/read",
        "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read"
    )
    NotActions = @()
    DataActions = @(
        "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read"
    )
    NotDataActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @("/subscriptions/<sub-id>")
}
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $customDataRole

# Important: DataActions are evaluated independently from Actions.
# An explicit deny assignment on a DataAction blocks it even if a role grants it.
# Use NotDataActions to subtract specific blob operations from a broader DataActions wildcard.

Key Vault has both control plane (create/delete/manage vault settings) and data plane (get/set/delete secrets, keys, certs) operations. The built-in Key Vault Secrets Officer role covers only data plane access.
SQL Managed Instance and Cosmos DB also expose DataActions for row-level and document-level access.
DataActions do not apply to classic (v1) storage accounts — only ARM-deployed storage supports them.
Inspecting and Comparing Role Definitions

# List all built-in roles
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object { $_.IsCustom -eq $false } |
    Select-Object Name, Description | Sort-Object Name | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Count of built-in roles in the current Azure environment
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object { $_.IsCustom -eq $false } | Measure-Object | Select-Object Count

# Find built-in roles that can write to Key Vault secrets (data plane)
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.DataActions -like '*KeyVault*secrets*write*' -or
    $_.DataActions -like '*KeyVault*secrets*set*'
} | Select-Object Name, DataActions

# Compare two built-in roles to see their differences
$owner = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Owner'
$contrib = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor'
Compare-Object $owner.NotActions $contrib.NotActions

# List all custom roles in the tenant
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Custom | Select-Object Name, Description, AssignableScopes

# Delete a custom role (only if no active assignments exist)
Remove-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'VM Operator' -Force
AssignableScopes in Depth

AssignableScopes defines where a role appears in the Azure portal role-picker and where it can be used in New-AzRoleAssignment. It does not determine what the role can do — that is determined entirely by Actions and DataActions. A role scoped to a single resource group can still be assigned there to grant access across every resource in that group.
A role with AssignableScopes: ['/'] is visible across the entire tenant and every subscription within it — use sparingly.
Scoping to a specific subscription prevents the role from appearing in other subscriptions' role pickers, reducing confusion.
Management group scope allows a single custom role to be available across dozens of member subscriptions without duplicating the definition.
You cannot assign a role at a scope broader than its AssignableScopes. If a role's AssignableScopes is /subscriptions/X, you cannot assign it at the tenant or management group level.

# AssignableScopes controls WHERE a role can appear in the assignment picker.
# It does NOT grant access — it only determines at which scopes an admin can assign it.

# Scope hierarchy (broadest to narrowest):
# /  (tenant root / management group root)
# /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<mg-id>
# /subscriptions/<sub-id>
# /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg-name>
# /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg-name>/providers/<type>/<name>

# Custom role scoped to a specific subscription only
$role = @{
    Name = "App Service Deployer"
    IsCustom = $true
    Description = "Deploy and restart App Service apps in prod"
    Actions = @(
        "Microsoft.Web/sites/read",
        "Microsoft.Web/sites/restart/action",
        "Microsoft.Web/sites/slots/read",
        "Microsoft.Web/sites/slots/slotsswap/action",
        "Microsoft.Web/sites/publishxml/action",
        "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read"
    )
    NotActions = @()
    DataActions = @()
    NotDataActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @(
        "/subscriptions/aaaa-bbbb-cccc-dddd",
        "/subscriptions/eeee-ffff-gggg-hhhh"
    )
}
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Scope to a management group (role available across all member subscriptions)
# Only Global Administrators who elevated access can assign at '/' scope
# To assign at management group scope:
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'App Service Deployer' `
  -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/contoso-prod-mg'

# Verify effective scope of an assignment (inherited vs direct)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId '<principal-id>' -IncludeClassicAdministrators |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope, ObjectType | Format-Table
Calculating Effective Permissions

A user's effective permissions at any scope are the union of all granted Actions across every role assignment that reaches that scope (directly assigned or inherited), minus the union of all NotActions. When a user has multiple role assignments at different scopes, Azure evaluates all of them together. The only way to truly block a permission regardless of roles is an explicit Deny Assignment.

# Calculate effective permissions for a user at a scope
# (union of all role assignments, then subtract all NotActions)
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'").Id
$scope = '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/prod-rg'

# Get all role assignments affecting this user at this scope (direct + inherited)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $userId -Scope $scope -ExpandPrincipalGroups |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope, ObjectType

# List the actual operations allowed at a scope (Azure permission check API)
# This is the what-if for a specific resource
$resourceId = '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm01'
Get-AzPermission -Scope $resourceId

# Check if a specific operation is permitted (useful in IaC validation)
# Using Azure CLI (more concise for permission testing)
az role assignment list --assignee alice@contoso.com --scope $resourceId `
    --query '[].roleDefinitionName' --output tsv

# Find all principals who have write access to a resource group
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg' |
    Where-Object { $_.RoleDefinitionName -in @('Owner','Contributor') } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, ObjectType
Evaluation Rule
Behavior
Multiple roles, same user
Actions are unioned. If Role A grants delete and Role B NotActions delete — delete IS permitted because Role A's grant wins.
Inherited vs direct assignment
No priority difference — both contribute to the effective set equally.
NotActions vs Deny Assignment
NotActions subtracts from a single role's Actions. A Deny Assignment blocks an action regardless of any role's grants.
DataActions independence
DataActions and Actions are evaluated in two separate passes. A role with Actions: * does NOT grant DataActions.
Role Definitions in Infrastructure as Code

Custom role definitions should be managed in version-controlled IaC alongside the resources that need them. Bicep and Terraform both support the Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions resource type. Using guid() with a stable seed ensures the role definition ID is deterministic and idempotent across deployments.

Always use guid(subscription().id, 'RoleName') or a similar deterministic seed when creating custom role definitions in IaC. A random ID would create a new role on every apply, leaving orphaned definitions in the tenant.
Governance and Housekeeping

Custom role definitions accumulate over time. Regular audits catch roles with excessively broad Actions, roles that were never adopted, and roles that lost their purpose as workloads changed. Build these checks into your quarterly RBAC review process.

# Detect overly broad custom roles (any role with Actions: '*')
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Custom | Where-Object { $_.Actions -contains '*' } |
    Select-Object Name, Description

# Find custom roles that have not been assigned to anyone (dead weight)
$customRoles = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Custom
$allAssignments = Get-AzRoleAssignment
$usedRoleIds = $allAssignments.RoleDefinitionId | Sort-Object -Unique
$customRoles | Where-Object { $_.Id -notin $usedRoleIds } |
    Select-Object Name, Id

# Count assignments per custom role (adoption check)
foreach ($role in $customRoles) {
    $count = ($allAssignments | Where-Object { $_.RoleDefinitionId -eq $role.Id }).Count
    [PSCustomObject]@{ Role = $role.Name; Assignments = $count }
} | Sort-Object Assignments -Descending

# Alert when a custom role is modified (Azure Activity Log)
# az monitor activity-log list with filter on operationName
az monitor activity-log list `
    --max-events 50 `
    --query "[?operationName.value=='Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/write']" `
    --output table
Summary
Role definitions are the permission blueprints in Azure RBAC. Key takeaways:
Actions / NotActions control the resource control plane; DataActions / NotDataActions control the data plane — they are evaluated independently.
NotActions subtracts from the effective allowed set but does NOT deny. Use Deny Assignments if a true block is needed.
The four foundational built-in roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader, User Access Administrator) cover most scenarios — prefer them before creating custom roles.
Custom roles enforce least privilege by specifying exactly the operations a workload or team needs, nothing more.
Clone an existing built-in role as a starting point to avoid missing operations your workload implicitly depends on.
Restrict AssignableScopes to the narrowest applicable scope and track custom role JSON in version control.
